text: The laparoscopic procedure involves making a longitudinal incision in the myometrium using bipolar scissors to access a 5 cm submucous myoma. The surgeon cuts through the myometrium until reaching the myoma's capsule. Hemostasis is achieved by applying traction to the myoma, followed by removal of connective tissue between the myoma and healthy myometrium. The incision may be extended if necessary, and the myoma is extracted using forceps with traction and counter-traction movements.

The surgeon carefully extracts the submucous myoma using forceps, ensuring the endometrium remains intact. The assistant aids in removing the myoma from its bed, avoiding energy application near the endometrium. Small bleeders surrounding the myoma are addressed, and the myoma is fully removed. Hemostasis is achieved by suturing any significant bleeders.

The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with 0 Vicryl to perform interrupted sutures on the myoma bed. The sutures are placed 2.5 centimeters outside the scar, invaginating healthy tissue into the myoma bed to decrease the risk of intrauterine hematoma. The endometrium is also incorporated into the sutures, and the knots are secured using intracorporeal knotting technique. The surgeon places three to four knots, ensuring strong cicatrization.

The surgeon uses minimal bipolar energy to preserve healthy tissue for optimal healing. Intracorporeal knotting is performed to invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed. The pelvis is rinsed to remove blood and reduce post-operative adhesion risks. The final knot is placed superiorly, avoiding the cornea, using the same intracorporeal knotting technique. The small myoma will be removed after knotting.

The surgeon achieves perfect coaptation between the two edges of the scar with no visible bleeding. The myoma is morcellated using a morcellator placed through the left lateral trochlea. The pelvis is visibly clean with no residual blood. A fascia closer is used to close the hole created by the morcellator, which is more than twelve millimeters, to reduce the risk of accidental bowel hernia.
